serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Chocolate Crunch Doorstop is approximately 40 g (one slice or piece), providing about 17 kcal, 4.1 g carbohydrate, and 4.0 g sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Chocolate Crunch Doorstop — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (cr_id G00010543)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo canonical record G00010543 for Chocolate Crunch Doorstop. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB source; set to -1 where not available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on milk chocolate bar (GI ~43–49) and crispy rice cereal (GI ~82), weighted by carbohydrate contribution
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Chocolate Crunch Doorstop' in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate components: (1) milk chocolate / cocoa-sugar matrix (GI ~43–49, University of Sydney GI Database) and (2) crispy rice cereal component (GI ~82, University of Sydney GI Database). Sugar constitutes 95.9% of total carbohydrate (9.94 g of 10.36 g per 100 g), which drives a higher blended GI estimate. Glycemic load per 40 g serving = (65 × 4.14 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.7 g (low GL per serving due to small serving size).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.94 g/100 g) with negligible potassium (5 mg/100 g) and zero dietary fibre; sugar-driven insulin-mediated sodium retention independently raises blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this high-sugar, fibre-free confection, as chronic high sugar intake promotes insulin resistance and sodium retention, independently elevating blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, even at the low absolute sodium level of 3 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar constitutes 95.9% of total carbohydrate (9.94 g sugar of 10.36 g total carbohydrate per 100 g) with zero dietary fibre, producing rapid postprandial glucose excursions with no fibre-mediated modulation; estimated GI 65 (medium-high for a sugar-dominant product)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this confection; the near-pure sugar carbohydrate profile with zero fibre drives rapid blood glucose spikes, and even a small 40 g portion delivers approximately 4.1 g of rapidly absorbed sugar with no nutritional offset.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Ultra-processed confection with high sugar (9.94 g/100 g), likely saturated fat from cocoa butter and butter components (saturated fat not reported in HPB source), and zero dietary fibre; consistent with dietary patterns associated with elevated CVD risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should minimise intake of this ultra-processed, high-sugar confection; the combination of free sugars, likely saturated fat from cocoa butter and dairy components, and absence of fibre or protective micronutrients is inconsistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns per AHA/ACC 2019 guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High free sugar (9.94 g/100 g) and likely saturated fat from butter and cocoa butter promote pro-inflammatory mediators; zero anti-inflammatory nutrients present in meaningful quantities
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this confection, as its high sugar and saturated fat content from cocoa butter and dairy components promote systemic inflammation, while the absence of omega-3 fatty acids, fibre, or polyphenols in meaningful quantities provides no anti-inflammatory offset.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chocolate Crunch Doorstop is a Western-style ultra-processed chocolate confection sold in Singapore, classified under Desserts / Cakes in the HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010543). It is a dense, crispy chocolate bar or slice made from a chocolate and sugar matrix — typically combining cocoa, cocoa butter, sugar, and dairy fat — set around a crispy rice cereal or wafer component that provides its characteristic crunchy texture. The product is extremely low in energy at only 42 kcal per 100 g, which is unusually low for a chocolate confection and suggests a high water or air content, or that the HPB record reflects a diluted or portion-adjusted preparation. The carbohydrate profile is dominated almost entirely by sugar: 9.94 g sugar of 10.36 g total carbohydrate per 100 g (95.9% sugar fraction), with zero dietary fibre. Protein is effectively absent (0.0 g/100 g, confirmed zero per HPB source), and fat is minimal at 0.25 g/100 g. Sodium is very low at 3 mg/100 g. Clinically, this food is of concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its near-pure sugar carbohydrate profile and estimated medium GI of 65, which drives rapid postprandial glucose excursions with no fibre moderation. It is also of concern for hypertension management due to high free sugar intake promoting insulin-mediated sodium retention, and for cardiovascular disease due to its ultra-processed nature and likely saturated fat from cocoa butter and dairy components. It is best consumed as an occasional small treat and is not suitable for regular consumption by individuals managing metabolic or cardiovascular conditions. Typical serving size is approximately 40 g (one piece or slice). Known in Singapore by descriptive names including Biskut Coklat Rangup (Malay) and 巧克力脆饼 (Chinese).

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo canonical record G00010543 with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input. The HPB Singapore database is an approved primary source for Singapore food records. Source match type is exact. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence for GI component only) as no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product.
notes: ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source G00010543 — this ultra-processed confection contains no protein-contributing ingredients in detectable quantities as formulated and reported. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — the product contains no fibre-contributing ingredients. magnesium_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source (0.0 mg/100 g). cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source — no cholesterol-contributing animal fat is detected at reportable levels in this formulation. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB Foodo source G00010543 for this product. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in HPB Foodo source G00010543 for this product as of 2026. ENERGY NOTE: The reported energy of 42 kcal/100 g is unusually low for a chocolate confection and is preserved as reported from the canonical HPB source without modification. This may reflect a specific product formulation, a diluted preparation, or a data entry characteristic of the HPB record. GI NOTE: GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for milk chocolate and crispy rice cereal components; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product. MULTILINGUAL NOTE: This is a Western-style confection sold in Singapore. Although the cuisine is classified as Western, the food is distributed in Singapore and local-language descriptive names have been provided for Chinese (巧克力脆饼 / 巧克力脆餅), Malay (Biskut Coklat Rangup), Tamil (சாக்லேட் க்ரஞ்ச் பிஸ்கட்), and Hindi (चॉकलेट क्रंच बिस्किट) to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance and Q8 alias coverage requirements. These are descriptive transliterations and translations rather than established traditional names, as this is a commercial confection product rather than a traditional dish.
